schools.summary: The ministers 'workers together with him' (synergountes, co-labourers with God, 1 Cor 3:9); the entreaty not to 'receive the grace of God in vain' (eis kenon, into emptiness) — not letting the offered salvation come to nothing by neglect or a profession without persevering holiness (Wesley).
Chrysostom: lest they grow supine, Paul again alarms and arouses them.

A parenthesis quoting Isa 49:8 (of the Messiah's kingdom) — God hears and succours in the 'accepted time' (euprosdektos); applied with a double 'behold, now' — the prophesied day of salvation has dawned, the present the accepted time; grace to be received now, not postponed.
Cyril of Jerusalem: the present is the season of confession — confess in an acceptable time and receive the heavenly treasure.

'Giving no offence' (proskopen, occasion of stumbling) in anything, 'that the ministry be not blamed' (momethe) — no occasion to despise or reject the gospel; the concern for the credit of the sacred office, not Paul's reputation merely, the minister's misconduct reproaching the message.

themes: that the ministry be not blamed

schools.summary: Paul commends himself 'as ministers of God' — not by show like the false teachers but by the tests of a true servant; 'much patience' heading the list as the comprehensive grace; the first triad of general trials (afflictions, necessities, distresses); every test of God's servants found in Paul (Calvin).

Specific trials — inflicted by others ('stripes' plegais, scourgings, Acts 16:23; 'imprisonments'; 'tumults' akatastasiais, mob-violence) and freely borne ('labours' toil; 'watchings' agrypniais, sleepless nights; 'fastings' hunger/want); the marks of fidelity, not a boast of merit.

From sufferings to inward graces — 'pureness' (purity of motive and life, chastity), 'knowledge' (of the divine mysteries), 'longsuffering,' 'kindness'; not self-produced but 'by the Holy Ghost' (source and credential); culminating in 'love unfeigned' (anypokrito, the chief fruit of the Spirit, Wesley).

The minister's equipment — 'the word of truth' (faithful, undiluted preaching), 'the power of God' (the divine energy converting and sanctifying, not human eloquence), 'the armour of righteousness on right and left' (a complete panoply, offensive and defensive, Eph 6:11) — fully furnished for the gospel warfare.

A series of paradoxes — 'honour and dishonour,' 'evil report and good report' (the world's shifting verdicts, through which the minister holds his course); the first great antithesis 'as deceivers, and yet true' — branded impostors by the world (its standing slander on true ministers, Wesley) yet genuine before God.

More paradoxes — 'unknown, yet well known' (obscure nobodies to the world, yet known to God and those who matter); 'dying, and behold we live' (exposed to death yet preserved); 'chastened, and not killed' (afflicted as under God's rod yet not destroyed, echoing Ps 118:18) — appearance of nothingness, reality of preservation.

The climax — 'sorrowful, yet alway rejoicing' (a man of grief, yet inward joy no sorrow quenches, Phil 4:4); 'poor, yet making many rich' (destitute, yet enriching multitudes with the riches of Christ); 'having nothing, yet possessing all things' (all is ours if we are Christ's, Wesley) — the gospel's reversal.

The rare tender address 'O ye Corinthians' (used seldom, marking deep emotion); 'our mouth is open' (aneoigen, stands open — he has kept nothing back) flowing from 'our heart is enlarged' (peplatyntai, expanded in love wide enough to embrace them all) — fullness of affection producing freedom of speech.

No 'straitness' in Paul (ou stenochoreisthe) — his affection roomy, with ample place for them; the narrowness 'in your own bowels' (splanchna, their affections grown contracted toward their apostle, perhaps under the false teachers); a tender reproach, the failure of fellowship on their side.

A plea for reciprocity — 'a recompence in the same' (antimisthian, a fair return in kind), that as he opened his heart they open theirs; 'as unto my children' (a father's right, having begotten them, 1 Cor 4:15) softening the reproof; asking nothing hard, only their love — 'be ye also enlarged.'

'Unequally yoked' (heterozygountes, from the law against yoking different beasts, Lev 19:19; Deut 22:10) — not bound in intimate union with unbelievers; especially marriage (Wesley), and sharing in idolatry (Calvin); the first two of five questions exposing incompatibility — righteousness/unrighteousness, light/darkness; not abandoning all contact but refusing binding alliances.

The starkest contrasts — 'concord' (symphonesis, a chord in unison) impossible between Christ and 'Belial' (Hebrew for worthlessness/vileness, applied to Satan, the NT's only use); 'what part hath he that believeth with an infidel?' — the believer, united to Christ, no common lot with the unbeliever of the opposite kingdom.

The climactic question — 'what agreement hath the temple of God with idols?' (none, as ark and Dagon); the great reason 'ye are the temple of the living God' (cf 1 Cor 3:16), in whom he dwells by his Spirit; the covenant promise woven from Scripture ('I will dwell in them... be their God'); God tolerating no idols under his own roof (Wesley).

themes: the temple of God and idols
'ye are the temple of the living God'

schools.summary: A threefold command from Isa 52:11 (the exiles leaving Babylon) — 'come out from among them,' 'be ye separate,' 'touch not the unclean thing' (akathartou); as the priests left Babylon's unclean things, so God's people the world's pollutions; sealed by the gracious promise 'I will receive you' — separation the condition of fuller communion.

A composite citation — 'I will be a Father' (2 Sam 7:14), 'sons and daughters' (Isa 43:6), 'saith the Lord Almighty' (pantokrator, Ruler of all, 2 Sam 7:8); the promise to David's house applied to all believers; the highest motive — those who forsake the unequal yoke gain the dignity of being children of the Almighty (Wesley).

themes: 'I will be a Father unto you'
